title: What Drives Variation in the U.S. Debt-to-Output Ratio? The Dogs that Did not Bark
abstract: A higher U.S. government debt-to-output (D-O) ratio does not forecast higher surpluses or lower returns on Treasurys in the future. Neither future cash flows nor discount rates account for the variation in the current D-O ratio. The market valuation of Treasurys is surprisingly insensitive to macro fundamentals. Instead, the future D-O ratio accounts for most of the variation because the D-O ratio is highly persistent. Systematic surplus forecast errors may help account for these findings. Since the start of the Global Financial Crisis, surplus projections have anticipated a large fiscal correction that failed to materialize.

article.introduction:
In light of large increasesin the government debt-to-output (D-O) ratios over the past 15 years, there is an ongoing debate in the United States and other advanced economies about fiscal sustainability (see, e.g., Croce, Nguyen, and Schmid (2012), Brunnermeier, Merkel, and Sannikov (2020), Chernov, Schmid, and Schneider (2020), Mian, Straub, and Sufi (2021), Reis (2021), Jiang et al. (2024)). Some economists point out that lower expected growth-adjusted returns on government debt—which can come from lower nominal returns, higher inflation, or higher real growth—can rationalize the recent increases in the U.S. D-O ratio (Blanchard (2019), Furman and Summers (2020), Cochrane (2022a)). Others argue that the run-up in debt could reflect expectations of higher future surpluses (Bohn (1998), Cochrane (2020)).
  The time-series dynamics of the government D-O ratio can shed light on this debate. On the one hand, if the D-O ratio is strongly mean-reverting, then the recent increase in the D-O ratio is a transitory issue that will self-correct, through larger surpluses, faster growth, or low returns. On the other hand, if the D-O ratio is close to a unit-root process, then the high D-O ratio can persist for a long time and may impact the government's ability to finance its future deficits, ultimately threatening the stability of bond and money markets.
  Unfortunately, standard univariate econometric tests of the stationarity of the D-O ratio are underpowered against close-to-unit-root alternatives. A similar problem is observed about the dividend-price ratio in the stock market. In this paper, we apply a systems approach, inspired by the Campbell and Shiller (1988) decomposition of the dividend-price ratio. According to such a decomposition, a higher D-O ratio today has to be followed by lower growth-adjusted returns, higher primary surplus-to-output ratios, or, absent adjustments in these fundamentals, higher D-O ratios in the future. At the 10-year horizon, this decomposition attributes little variation in the D-O ratio in the postwar U.S. sample to variations in future fundamentals. Rather, the future D-O ratio 10 years from now accounts for nearly all of the variation in today's D-O ratio. Put differently, the D-O ratio is close to a unit root.
  This result suggests that a fast decline in the D-O ratio due to higher future surpluses or lower future returns is unlikely. Compared with the standard econometric tests of the unit root, our approach exploits the economic restrictions imposed by the present-value relationship between surpluses, returns, and the government debt level. In particular, the absence of surplus and return predictability implies predictability of the current D-O ratio for the future D-O ratio. This approach is in the spirit of Cochrane (2008), who argues that the absence of cash-flow growth predictability (the dog that did not bark) provides powerful evidence for return predictability in the stock market, even though such evidence is hard to establish in univariate tests. In the language of that paper,neitherthe cash flow nor the return dog barks in our setting. This bolsters the case for the near-unit-root behavior of government debt.
  In this way, our approach delivers a model-free test of fiscal sustainability defined as the rate of mean reversion in the D-O ratio driven by adjustments in future returns or surpluses. Our finding differs from prior literature. In particular, studying a shorter sample that ends in the mid-1990s, Bohn (1998) finds that primary surpluses increase when the D-O ratio is high. Cochrane (2022a,2022b) finds that the D-O ratio predicts nominal returns on the government debt portfolio. We confirm that a higher D-O ratio predicts significantly lower nominal rates, but we show that it also predicts lower inflation, offsetting the effect on nominal rates. We find no evidence that the D-O ratio predicts surpluses nor real growth-adjusted returns in our baseline post-WWII sample. Results from a longer sample that starts in 1842 confirm the post-WWII results. Bond prices today appear to be unresponsive to news about future fundamentals, that is, government surpluses and real growth-adjusted returns.
  We reach a different conclusion because of a statistical inference challenge. This issue is well-known in the asset pricing literature (Stambaugh (1999)), and at least as relevant in our context. Specifically, a small-sample bias affects the slope coefficients of the return and surplus predictability regressions, leading one to overstate their degree of predictability. The issue arises because of the combination of (i) the high persistence of the D-O ratio, that is, the predictor, and (ii) the high correlation between innovations to the predictor and the predicted variables (surpluses and returns). An increase in bond risk premia will tend to lower realized returns, the dependent variable, and the market value of the D-O ratio, the predictor. As a result, the expectation of the residual in the return predictability regression conditional on the D-O ratio cannot be zero, violating the ordinary least squares (OLS) assumptions. Because of this mechanical link between realized returns and the predictor, OLS will find evidence of return predictability even where there is none. The same small-sample bias affects the surplus predictability regressions. Governments must issue more debt when they run large deficits. The negative correlation between the innovation in the D-O ratio and the surplus similarly produces spurious evidence of surplus predictability. The bias-corrected slope coefficients imply substantially weaker surplus and return predictability than what is indicated by the OLS estimates.
  Despite the clear evidence in terms of the bias-adjusted point estimates, the standard errors point to the need to interpret the results with caution. The confidence intervals (CIs) for the return and surplus predictability coefficients are wider at longer horizons, implying that we cannot rule out some role for fundamentals at the long horizon, but the point estimates suggest a minor role. Large standard errors result from the low power of unit-root tests in short samples against close-to-unit-root alternatives (see Schwert (1987) and Lo and MacKinlay (1989) in finance). CIs shrink for a longer sample that starts in 1842, strengthening the evidence against debt adjustment via growth-adjusted returns. Statistical evidence against the discount rate channel is strong at all horizons. Even for this long sample, standard errors on the surplus predictability coefficients remain substantial. Hence, we cannot rule out some role for the cash-flow channel at long horizons. Given the higher short-run volatility of surpluses than of Treasury returns, what we can say is that if there is mean reversion in the D-O ratio, then debt adjustment is more likely to occur via adjustment of surpluses than via adjustment of real growth-adjusted returns. There is no statistical evidence that justifies the recent increase in the U.S. D-O ratio with a lower future cost of capital for the government or with expectations of higher future economic growth.
  Taken together, the evidence indicates that the D-O ratio may be subject to highly persistent innovations. As corroborating evidence, we simulate data from a model under the null hypothesis that the D-O ratio follows a near-unit-root process. This model exactly replicates our empirical findings. In particular, we find spurious evidence of surplus and return predictability using unadjusted OLS slope estimates. The bias-corrected estimates recover the true values of essentially no predictability.1
  One type of highly persistent shock to the D-O ratio is a structural break. There may have been a structural break in the relation between the D-O ratio, growth-adjusted returns, and surpluses. In stock markets, there also is evidence of structural breaks in the relation between the dividend yield, stock returns, and dividend growth (see Lettau and Van Nieuwerburgh (2008), Smith and Timmermann (2020)).2We estimate a structural break in the D-O ratio in 2007: the mean D-O ratio is0.81$0.81$log points higher after 2007 than before 2007. We define the transitory component of the D-O ratio as the raw D-O series minus the different subsample mean before and after 2007. Using this transitory D-O series, we find stronger evidence for surplus predictability but not return predictability. Fundamentals account for about 50% of the variation in the transitory component of the D-O ratio at the 10-year horizon. Removing a structural break removes a low-frequency component in the D-O ratio and creates more room for fundamentals in explaining the now-more-transitory nature of the variation in the D-O ratio. The resulting transitory D-O ratio is less persistent, and predictive coefficients have smaller small-sample biases.
  Importantly, this analysis leaves the large, permanent increase in the D-O ratio (as well as its timing) unexplained. In the last part of the paper, we propose three potential explanations for the structural break in the D-O ratio.
  Surplus forecast errors are a first potential contributor to the low-frequency change in the relationship between debt and fundamentals around 2007. While an econometrician with access to the U.S. sample does not predict higher surpluses or lower returns when the D-O ratio rises, bond investors may do so under their subjective beliefs. If bond investors systematically overpredict surpluses (or underpredict returns) as the D-O ratio increases, this could impute a unit root in the D-O ratio under the actual measure while generating a stationary D-O ratio and surplus and/or return predictability under the subjective measure. We provide evidence from Congressional Budget Office (CBO) projections of primary surpluses that systematically exceed realized surpluses after 2007, imputing more mean reversion to the predicted D-O ratio under the subjective than under the actual measure.3For example, in 2010, well after the Global Financial Crisis (GFC), the CBO projections of 10-year cumulative surpluses exceeded realized surpluses by 5.4% of GDP per annum. Under investors' subjective measure, more of the variation in the D-O ratio can be attributed to perceived future surpluses. Optimistic beliefs result in mispricing of U.S. government debt.
  A second candidate explanation for the low-frequency change in the relationship between debt and fundamentals is the rise in foreign and central bank purchases of U.S. Treasurys. Removing foreign and Federal Reserve holdings of Treasuries results in a particularly large adjustment after 2007. If we use the remaining domestic private-sector holdings of debt scaled by GDP as the predictor, we find larger surplus predictability, similar to the evidence that uses the transitory component of the D-O ratio as predictor. This suggests that inelastic demand by the Fed and the rest of the world may be a contributing factor to the insensitivity of government bond prices to fundamentals. Large purchases of government bonds by central banks to help governments finance wars have been cited as instances of financial repression, that is, measures undertaken by governments to suppress the government's cost of borrowing (Reinhart, Kirkegaard, and Sbrancia (2011), Hall and Sargent (2022)). The recent increase in the size of central bank balance sheets can be interpreted as a new wave of financial repression.
  Finally, a change in fiscal policy may have contributed to the structural break. We uncover a decline in the responsiveness of the surplus to the D-O ratio, which turns negative after 2007. This result suggests that fiscal policy became more permissive in recent times, contributing to the increase in the D-O ratio. With surpluses not contributing to fiscal adjustment, or even aggravating the situation, we find that inflation contributes more to the adjustment in this period. Indeed, the D-O ratio predicts inflation with a positive sign in the post-2007 sample. These results are consistent with what Corhay et al. (2023) refer to as a switch from a monetary to a fiscal regime.
  Our paper builds on the growing literature that examines the predictability of returns in asset markets. There is substantial evidence that stock returns are predictable (see Koijen and Van Nieuwerburgh (2011) for a survey of the literature on stock return predictability). When valuations are high in stock markets, they revert back to the mean mostly through lower subsequent returns (Campbell and Thompson (2007), Cochrane (2008)), in part through higher dividend growth (van Binsbergen and Koijen (2010), Golez and Koudijs (2018)). Overall, the discount rates on stocks are remarkably volatile (Hansen and Jagannathan (1991)), and the valuation of stocks seems excessively volatile compared to its fundamentals (LeRoy and Porter (1981), Shiller (1981)). This evidence that high valuations revert to the mean through low subsequent returns is common across most asset classes. This does not hold, however, for the U.S. government bond portfolio. Even though there is evidence of return predictability for individual bonds (see Fama and Bliss (1987), Campbell and Shiller (1991), Cochrane and Piazzesi (2005), Ludvigson and Ng (2009), Cochrane (2011)), we find no evidence that lower returns on the entire bond portfolio push the D-O ratio back to its long-run mean following a high value. The fluctuations in the market value of the entire U.S. government bond portfolio are too small compared to the fluctuations in its fundamentals—there is excess smoothness as opposed to excess volatility in the stock market. The cash flows and the discount rates are the dogs that did not bark when there is news about future surpluses or returns. Instead, we only find a statistically significant role for future debt in accounting for the entire value of debt today.
  Our evidence on forecast errors in the surplus-to-GDP ratio connects to the growing literature on the role of subjective beliefs in asset pricing (Barberis (2018), Bordalo et al. (2019), Bordalo et al. (2020)). For the stock market, De La O and Myers (2021) and Wang (2020) impute a much larger share of variation in the price-dividend ratio to the cash-flow component under subjective than under objective beliefs, consistent with our findings. Mistakes in interest rate and growth forecasts also contribute to our findings. Piazzesi and Schneider (2009) and Cieslak (2018) document evidence on the gap between statistical risk premia measured by econometricians and subjective risk premia expected by investors in the bond market.
  Our paper does not impose no-arbitrage restrictions, but rather uses accounting identities to understand variation in the D-O ratio, instead of the level. In other work, Jiang et al. (2024) price a claim to future surpluses in a no-arbitrage model, and conclude that the government bond portfolio is overpriced. In no-arbitrage models, all of the information for forecasting bond returns is embedded in the yield curve, except when the underlying factors cannot be inverted from the yields (see Duffee (2011)). Bauer and Hamilton (2018) conclude that the evidence for macro variables predicting bond returns, after controlling for bond yields, against the spanning hypothesis is weaker than previously thought, citing similar small-sample biases in the presence of persistent predictors.
  A large literature on the pitfalls of predicting returns with persistent predictors goes back to Nelson and Kim (1993), Stambaugh (1999), Lewellen (2004), Torous, Valkanov, and Yan (2004), Campbell and Yogo (2006), and Boudoukh, Israel, and Richardson (2022). The Stambaugh correction can be refined if the econometrician rules out ex ante that the predictor variable has a true autocorrelation larger than one (Lewellen (2004)).4We do not rule out nonstationary behavior of the D-O ratio because we are interested in testing fiscal sustainability. We implement the Campbell and Yogo (2006) testing procedure, valid under general assumptions, which confirms that there is evidence of neither surplus nor return predictability.
  While our paper finds that the U.S. D-O ratio is highly persistent and close to a unit root, we rule out an actual unit root in the D-O ratio because it is hard to reconcile with the historical record of the United States and other countries (Chen et al. (2022)), and because it seems less plausible on economic grounds.5In subsequent work, Campbell, Gao, and Martin (2023) assume that the U.S. D-O ratio has an actual unit root. They propose a different predictor, the surplus-to-debt ratio, and show that it is less persistent and predicts future surpluses. However, this metric has a different economic interpretation. It is mean-reverting because its denominator—government debt—is increasing over time, which stabilizes the growing deficits. In contrast, it is precisely the increase in debt that drives near-unit-root behavior in the D-O ratio: To obtain mean reversion in the D-O ratio, the government needs to pay down the debt. To avoid this undesirable feature, Campbell, Gao, and Martin (2023) rely on a log-linear approximation. But when the D-O ratio is nonstationary, this approximation of the surplus-to-debt ratio no longer depends on the valuation of debt itself (AppendixF).
  The paper is organized as follows. SectionIderives and estimates the decomposition of the D-O ratio, with and without small-sample bias adjustment. SectionIIconsiders highly persistent shocks to the D-O ratio in the form of a structural break. SectionIIalso considers three economic mechanisms that may have contributed to such a structural break. SectionIIIconcludes.
article.I. What Drives Variation in the U.S. Deb:
We use the log-linear Campbell and Shiller (1988) decomposition of the government's D-O ratio in Cochrane (2022a,2022b). This approach decomposes the variation in the D-O ratio into three components: expected future government surpluses (cash flows), expected future real growth-adjusted bond returns (discount rates), and the expected future value of the D-O ratio. After we correct for the Stambaugh (1999) bias, we find that most of the variation in the D-O ratio cannot be attributed to cash flows or discount rates over the next 10 years.
  We compute the market value of all marketable U.S. Treasurys using data from CRSP Treasurys. Following Hall and Sargent (2011), we compute the return on government debt as the sum of the principal and coupon payments less new issuance, plus the market value at the end of the period, divided by the market value at the end of the previous period. We exclude nonmarketable debt, which is mostly held in intragovernmental accounts. The inflation rate is the annual log change in the Consumer Price Index (CPI), taken from the Bureau of Labor Statistics. Nominal gross domestic product (GDP) is from the Bureau of Economic Analysis. Our sample of annual data comprises the period from 1947 to 2022.
  Letrt+j$r_{t+j}$denote the nominal return on the government debt portfolio (in logs),xt+j$x_{t+j}$real GDP growth (in logs), andπt+j$\pi _{t+j}$log inflation. Letr∼t+j=rt+j−xt+j−πt+j$\widetilde{r}_{t+j}=r_{t+j} -x_{t+j}-\pi _{t+j}$denote the adjusted log bond return, which is the nominal return on the government bond portfolio minus output growth and inflation. We refer tor∼$\widetilde{r}$as the “real growth-adjusted return” or simply the “return” when there is no risk of confusion. Letst+j=syt+j/exp(v)$s_{t+j}=sy_{t+j}/\text{exp}(v)$denote the adjusted government surplus-to-output ratio, wheresyt$sy_t$denotes the actual surplus-to-output ratio andv$v$denotes the average log D-O ratio.
  TableIreports the decade-by-decade averages for these variables. The evidence forr<x+π$r<x+\pi$is concentrated in the first half of the sample. The real growth-adjusted returns on the entire bond portfolio, reported in the first column, are strongly negative in the 1950s (−3.8%), due to low real returns and strong economic growth. This pattern continues in the 1960s, with real growth-adjusted returns of −2.8%. In the 1970s, bondholders were surprised by persistently high inflation, delivering growth-adjusted returns of −2.5%. Overall, in the first three decades after WWII, the average gap between returns and growthr∼$\tilde{r}$is −3.5%, withr$r$much lower thanx+π$x+\pi$. In the next four decades, the average real growth-adjusted returnr∼$\tilde{r}$is positive (1.3%), withr$r$higher thanx+π$x+\pi$. The 1980s represents a radical departure from what came before. Bondholders earned high real returns despite high inflation (4.1%). Even in the 1990s and 2000s, bondholders earned high real growth-adjusted returns of 1.6% and 0.8%, respectively. Since 2010, growth-adjusted returns have turned negative again.
  Surpluses, reported in the last column, are modestly positive in the 1950s, 1960s, and 1990s, and negative in the four other decades. Surpluses are most negative over the last 20 years. Over the entire postwar sample, we see that the “debt deflation” from negative real growth-adjusted returnsr∼$\tilde{r}$of around −1% per year is more than offset by the deficits in excess of 1% per year. From a backward-looking perspective, this difference accounts for the rise in the D-O ratio in-sample. In this paper, we take a forward-looking perspective and study how debt dynamics affect the variation in long-run future surpluses and returns shown in TableI.
  Figure1plots the variables we want to forecast: the real growth-adjusted returnsr∼t$\tilde{r}_t$in the left panel and the surplusst$s_t$in the right panel. As discussed above, returns were low from the start of the sample until the 1980s. After 2000, real growth-adjusted returns declined again. The surplusess$s$are high in the 1950s and 1990s and decline precipitously in the last two decades. We note that the surplusst$s_t$, which is backed out from equation (1), has a high correlation of 78% with the raw surplus-to-output ratio. The implied surplusst$s_t$is more volatile (standard deviation of 6.40%) than the actual surplus-to-output ratios (standard deviation of 2.74%).
  The solid line in Figure2is the predictor variable, the demeaned log D-O ratio. There was a marked decline in the D-O ratio between 1948 and 1974, followed by a gradual increase in the 1980s and early 1990s, and after a brief period of decline, a new surge in the wake of the GFC (after 2007).
  The decomposition in equation (2) allows for the presence of convenience yields on government debt.8Convenience yields reducer∼$\widetilde{r}$.
  To develop intuition for how the persistence in the D-O ratio impacts the variance decomposition, consider the case of an AR(1) process for the D-O ratio with persistence parameterϕ1$\phi _1$. The variance decomposition is then given byb1s(1−ϕ1)T/(1−ϕ1)$b^s_1 (1-\phi _1)^T/(1-\phi _1)$,−b1r(1−ϕ1)T/(1−ϕ1)$-b^r_1 (1-\phi _1)^T/(1-\phi _1)$, andϕ1T$\phi _1^T$. As the D-O ratio approaches a unit-root process (ϕ1→1$\phi _1 \rightarrow 1$), the share ofvt+T$v_{t+T}$in the variation invt$v_t$approaches 100%.
  We define fiscal sustainability over a finite horizonT$T$asϕT<1$\phi _T<1$. We define fiscal sustainability over long horizons aslimT→∞ϕT=0$\lim _{T\rightarrow \infty }\phi _T=0$. If this condition is not satisfied, then the surplus-to-output ratio will inherit a unit root, which means that surpluses can drift arbitrarily far from output, which is not fiscally sustainable.
  In the second case, surpluses are unpredictable. If the surpluses are i.i.d., then returns must adjust to bring the D-O ratio back down after an increase:−bTr=1−ϕT>0$-b^r_T=1-\phi _T>0$. However, given the empirical constellation of short-run variances of surpluses and returns, the stationarity of the D-O ratio would imply long-run meanaversionin returns, which is at odds with the data.
  This back-of-the-envelope evidence on the relative volatility of surpluses versus returns seems to rule out a world with a stationary D-O ratio and no surplus predictability, but potentially allows for a world with a stationary D-O ratio and no return predictability.11
  If there is no predictability of surpluses nor of real growth-adjusted returns, then the D-O ratio inherits a unit root, which violates fiscal sustainability.
  Before we estimate the variance decomposition of the D-O ratio in the data, we briefly digress and perform a range of univariate tests to try to determine whether the log D-O ratio is stationary. AppendixCconsiders seven different tests of the null of a unit root, as well as a state-of-the-art test of the null of a stationary process for the log D-O ratio. For the postwar sample (1947 to 2022), we cannot reject either null hypothesis. The findings for a long prewar period (1842 to 1940) are similar. This conclusion may not be surprising in light of the difficulties that univariate tests face in distinguishing a unit root from a near-unit-root process. The stock return predictability literature has uncovered similar issues when studying the persistence of the log dividend-price ratio on the aggregate stock market. If we take a broader historical perspective, there is a lot more evidence of mean reversion. Based on the fiscal experience of the Dutch Republic in the 17thand 18thcenturies and the United Kingdom in the 19thcentury (Chen et al. (2022)), both of these countries pushed the D-O ratio well above 150% to finance large wars, and their market value of the D-O subsequently declined dramatically once they lost the privilege of being the world's safe asset supplier. It is also worth emphasizing that it seems less plausible for an economic model to impute a unit root to the market value of debt by output ratio.12The inconclusive result of unit-root tests opens the door for our multivariate approach, which simultaneously considers log D-O, the surplus-to-GDP ratio, and the growth-adjusted return on the government debt portfolio.
  Bond investors care about the government's ability to roll over the debt. Given that the average maturity of the U.S. government debt portfolio has hovered around five to seven years, and only about 13% of outstanding government debt has maturity longer than 10 years,13the U.S. Treasury will typically roll over the entire debt in less than a decade. Hence, studying predictability at horizons up to 10 years is natural.
  Figure3plots the baseline OLS regression results. Each panel plots the OLS slope coefficients in (4), where the dependent variable is future surpluses (top left), future real growth-adjusted returns (top right), and future D-O ratios (bottom left) for horizons fromT=1$T=1$toT=10$T=10$years. The bottom right panel has surpluses minus growth-adjusted returns as the dependent variable. We refer to this dependent variable as “fundamentals.” TableIIreports these same predictability results in tabular format.
  At the one-year horizon, 99% of the variance is attributed to the next year's D-O ratio. That is to say, the log D-O ratio is highly persistent. The first-order autocorrelation is 0.99. Even at the five-year horizon, 88% of the D-O ratio fluctuations can be attributed to the future D-O ratio. TheR2$R^2$in this D-O predictability regression exceeds50%$50\%$at the five-year horizon. At the 10-year horizon, the other two channels start to matter: 23% of the variation is attributed to fluctuations in expected future surpluses and 27% of the variation is attributed to fluctuations in expected future returns. The terminal D-O ratio in the 10-year horizon accounts for the remaining 49% of the variation.
  Why is the discount rate channel so weak? The D-O ratio does predict nominal bond returns with the right sign, as emphasized by Cochrane (2022a): higher D-O predicts lower nominal returns. This effect is mechanical because bond prices, which enter in the D-O ratio, are determined by the nominal log returns over the maturity of the bond:ptN=−∑i=0N−1hprt+i+1N−i$p^N_t=-\sum ^{N-1}_{i=0} hpr^{N-i}_{t+i+1}$. However, the predictability effect on nominal returns is offset almost completely by a similar-sized predictability effect on inflation, as shown in the third panel of TableIII. Higher debt leads to lower inflation in the full postwar sample, a surprising result. The net effect of the D-O ratio on future real growth-adjusted returns, shown in the bottom panel, is small and statistically insignificant. We return to this interesting decomposition in SectionII.B.3.
  The bootstrapped standard errors are under the null that the D-O ratio is stationary and there potentially is a role for fundamentals to affect the debt dynamics. We find the coefficientsψ1$\psi _1$andψ2$\psi _2$that minimize the mean squared distance between the local projections of the future log D-O ratios in the next 1 through 10 periods on the current log D-O ratio in the simulated small samples and in the data. Similarly, we find the coefficientsβ1r$\beta ^r_1$andβ2r$\beta ^r_2$that minimize the mean squared distance between the local projections of the future growth-adjusted returns in the next 1 through 10 periods on the current log D-O ratio in the simulated small samples and in the data. This procedure allows us to take the small-sample bias into account. Including two lags of the D-O ratio in the data-generating process generates a better fit to the data than a model with one lag and delivers white noise residuals to bootstrap from.
  The CIs imply considerable uncertainty about the point estimate. Even at the 10-year horizon, the two-standard-deviation CIs for theb10s$b^s_{10}$andb10r$b^r_{10}$coefficients contain zero, so that the explanatory power of future surpluses and growth-adjusted returns are indistinguishable from zero. The CIs for the coefficients in the growth-adjusted return predictability equation are substantially smaller than for the coefficients in the surplus predictability equation.
  The high persistence in the explanatory variablevt$v_t$raises the concern of small-sample bias. The OLS slope coefficients in the return predictability relationship,|bjr|$|b^r_j|$, are biased upward in absolute value because the innovations to the returnsεr$\varepsilon ^r$are positively correlated with the regressor innovationsεv$\varepsilon ^v$, and the regressor is highly persistent (Stambaugh (1999)). Intuitively, an increase in bond risk premia will tend to induce lower realized returns and lower the ratio of the market value of debt to output. That gives rise to positive correlations between the regressor innovations and the return innovations. The positive biases tend to increase for long-horizon predictability regressions (Boudoukh, Israel, and Richardson (2022)).
  We find a similar positive bias for the surplus predictability regression. An increase (decrease) in debt issuance tends to coincide with the government running large deficits (surpluses). As a result, there is a strong, negative correlation betweenεs$\varepsilon ^s$and the regressor innovationsεv$\varepsilon ^v$. This, combined with the persistence of the D-O ratio, induces a large upward bias inbTs$b^s_T$as well.
  Figure4reports the bias-adjusted regression coefficients. TableIIreports the bias-corrected coefficient estimates in the rows labeledunbiased. The three predictability coefficients still sum to one. The bias-corrected variance decomposition attributes −2% and −11% of the D-O ratio variance to the discount rate and cash flow channel, respectively, at the five-year horizon. As a result, 113% is accounted for by the future D-O ratio. At the 10-year horizon, we still attribute 101% of the variance to the future D-O ratio, after correcting for the small-sample bias. Variation in future surpluses over the next 10 years accounts for −6% and future returns for 4% of the variation in the D-O ratio. The latter two numbers are far below the 23% and 27% OLS point estimates, showing a very large small-sample bias. The bias-corrected point estimates combined with the standard errors imply that there is no evidence that the D-O ratio predicts future government surpluses or future bond returns. The null hypothesis that there is no predictability in future government surpluses or future bond returns cannot be rejected at any horizon. At the 10-year horizon, we cannot even reject the joint null that the D-O ratio does not predict fundamentals, the difference between surpluses and growth-adjusted returns. This is the main result in the paper.
  The CIs for the cash flow and discount rate contributions in the top left and right panels of Figure4are substantial. We cannot rule out that cash flows and discount rates play a quantitatively meaningful role in imputing mean reversion to the D-O ratio at the 10-year horizon. Even though the point estimates are close to zero, the 95% CI almost covers values of 30% for cash flows and 50% for discount rates at the 10-year horizon. Similarly, we cannot definitively rule out that there is significant mean reversion at the 10-year horizon, even though the point estimate for the futureV$V$contribution, shown in the bottom left panel, is close to 100%. We have low power at longer horizons, consistent with the evidence from the unit-root tests discussed above.
  Our evidence is consistent with a near-unit root in the D-O ratio. To provide further evidence on this hypothesis, we simulate data under the null of the near-unit-root process for the log D-O ratio in (6).15Under this null, there are only minor contributions from return or surplus predictability. We simulate 10,000 samples with the same length as the actual sample. We then estimate the forecasting regressions (4) by OLS in each simulated sample. We plot the mean slope coefficients and the two-standard-deviation CIs around these mean slope coefficient estimates.
  In the simulated data, we find that future government surpluses and discount rates appear to explain a nontrivial fraction of variation in the D-O ratio in small samples, even though they do not by assumption. Figure5reports the variance decomposition implied by the simulated samples (red lines). The average OLS slope coefficients generated by the near-unit-root model imply variance decompositions that are very close to our point estimates from the historical sample in Figure3. The OLS estimates for the autoregressive coefficient are severely biased downward in small samples when the true model has a near-unit root ((Hamilton,1994, p. 217)). As a result, we find spurious evidence of mean reversion that creates a large role for fundamentals over longer horizons, in cases in which there is little to no mean reversion. The true slope coefficientsϕT$\phi _T$are close to one at all horizonsT$T$up until at least 10 years.
  Quantitatively, the simulation suggests that the downward bias in the variance explained by the future log D-O ratiovt+T$v_{t+T}$is about 50% at the 10-year horizon. If we adjust the variance decomposition result in Figure3, the bias-adjusted variance explained by the future log D-O ratio is about49%+50%=99%$49\%+50\%=99\%$at the 10-year horizon. This number is close to the 101% bias-corrected number we obtained in Figure4and TableII. In sum, the simulation exercise provides corroborating evidence for the presence of a near-unit root in the log D-O ratio.
  We compute both coefficient standard errors and small-sample biases via bootstrap. We draw 10,000 bootstrap samples of the residuals(uv,ur)$(u^v,u^r)$with replacement, generating artificial samples of the same length as the data from (6). We estimate the coefficients in (7) to (9) in each small sample. Our bias adjustments are the distance between the mean regression coefficientsbTs$b^s_T$,bTr$b^r_T$, andϕT$\phi _T$in small samples and their true values estimated from a very long sample.
  Figure6reports the coefficient estimates forbTs$b^s_T$,bTr$b^r_T$, andϕT$\phi _T$in (7) to (9), with (blue line) and without (red line) the bias adjustment. Similar to the baseline case, without the bias adjustment, future government surpluses and returns seem to explain a nonnegligible fraction of the variation in the log D-O ratio. This is no longer true with the bias adjustment. The future D-O ratio explains nearly 100% of the variation in the log D-O ratio at the 10-year horizon.
  Standard return predictability tests, such as thet$t$-test of the slope of the OLS predictability coefficient, may be inappropriate when the predictor is persistent and its innovations are highly correlated with returns. In such cases, large-sample theory provides a poor approximation to the finite-sample distribution of tests statistics. Campbell and Yogo (2006) offer an alternative test that is valid under general assumptions about the predictor dynamics, even when the largest root is larger than one, and general assumptions on the distribution of innovations. Campbell and Yogo (2006) develop a pretest to diagnose whether the conventionalt$t$-test leads to valid inference. Their Dickey-Fueller generalized least squares (DF-GLS) test is based on the CI for the largest autoregressive root of the predictor variable. Applying their method to our context, we obtain a 95% CI of[0.959,1.059]$[0.959, 1.059]$for the persistence of the D-O variablevt$v_t$. Since this CI contains the unit root, this indicates that standardt$t$-tests are not valid.
  Campbell and Yogo (2006) go on to develop an asymptotically valid and efficientQ$Q$-test, which results in a Bonferroni CI for the predictive coefficient of interest. When we apply their procedure to the surplus predictability regression, we find a 90% CI of[$[$-0.062,0.005]$0.062, 0.005]$. This CI includes zero, so we fail to reject the null hypothesis that the lagged D-O ratiovt$v_t$does not forecast the surplus-to-output ratiost+1$s_{t+1}$(H0:b1s=0$H_0: b^s_1=0$). We repeat the analysis for the adjusted return on the debt portfolio,r∼t+1$\widetilde{r}_{t+1}$, and find a 90% Bonferroni CI of[0.000,0.057]$[0.000, 0.057]$. We fail to reject the null hypothesis that the lagged D-O ratio does not forecast the government debt return (H0:b1r=0$H_0: b^r_1=0$). This analysis confirms our findings: once the high degree of persistence of the D-O ratio is taken into account, evidence for predictability of future surpluses or future returns by the lagged D-O ratio is very weak.
  We test the robustness of our results to considering a longer sample, revisiting the shorter Bohn (1998) sample, and considering different steady-state values for the adjusted returns on the debt portfolio.
  We extend the sample in an effort to improve the power of our tests. Using the Hall et al. (2018) historical data, we construct a longer U.S. sample that starts in 1842. The variance decomposition with and without bias adjustment is plotted in Figure7. The bias-corrected point estimates are similar to those obtained for the post-WWII sample. The longer sample helps shrink the CIs around the point estimates. The shrinkage in CIs is pronounced for the growth-adjusted return predictability coefficients, but only modest for the surplus predictability coefficients. We can strongly reject the conjecture that growth-adjusted returns contribute nontrivially to debt adjustment. Surplus adjustment accounts for about 20% of the variability in the D-O ratio, according to the bias-adjusted point estimate. While this is higher than in the postwar sample, we still cannot reject the null hypothesis that there is no surplus predictability. At the 10-year horizon, we can now definitively rule out that futurev$v$accounts for less than 45% of the variation. Conversely, we can rule out that fundamentals account for more than 45% of the variation in the D-O ratio. After the bias correction,v$v$10 years out explains 81.7% of the variation in today'sv$v$based on the point estimates.
  The longer data sample allows us to extend the predictability horizon further. Since we have only nine independent observations of 20-year samples in the 180-year time series, CIs naturally grow with the horizon. FigureD2shows results for up to 25 years. The point estimates indicate no role for growth-adjusted returns and a larger role for surpluses, with biased-adjusted point estimates around 35% at the 25-year horizon. Given the large CIs, we still cannot reject the null hypothesis that surpluses are not predictable. In short, we continue to find strong evidence against return predictability and weak evidence for surplus predictability.
  In a classic paper on this topic, Bohn (1998) finds evidence that the D-O ratio predicts surpluses and interprets this evidence as against the unit-root hypothesis. Bohn considers several samples that all end in 1995, and his main results are robust across samples. We reestimate our results for the two main Bohn samples: 1916 to 1995 and 1948 to 1995. Before correcting for the small-sample bias, our methodology also identifies a larger role for fundamentals in the samples ending in 1995, as shown in FiguresD3andD4, consistent with Bohn's findings. However, this evidence mostly disappears after the Stambaugh bias correction.
  Since we use the same persistent predictorvt$v_t$on the right-hand side, we apply the same Stambaugh-bias adjustment formula as in the main text. FigureD5plots the estimates with and without bias adjustment forρ=exp(1%)$\rho =\exp (1\%)$. FigureD6plots the estimates forρ=exp(−1%)$\rho =\exp (-1\%)$. The results after the bias correction for both cases are similar to those obtained for the benchmarkρ=0$\rho =0$case.
article.II. Structural Break in the Mean D-O Rat:
One way to allow for highly persistent shocks to the D-O ratio, consistent with the near-unit-root evidence, is to allow for a structural break. There may have been structural shifts in the relation between the valuation of debt and fundamentals. Specifically, a major contributor to the small role of fundamentals is the large run-up in government debt during the GFC that was not followed by commensurate increases in surpluses or decreases in returns. We find evidence for a structural break in the mean of the log D-O ratio around the GFC. We then consider three economic mechanisms that could have induced this structural break.
  We consider a structural break in the mean of the log D-O ratio in 2007. A Chow test withF$F$-statistic 8.09 for structural breakpoints rejects the null hypothesis of no structural break at the 0.58% level in 2007. The structural break introduces a0.81$0.81$(in log scale) increase in the mean D-O ratio. Obviously, when we allow for this break, we introduce permanent innovations in the D-O ratio. Following Lettau and Van Nieuwerburgh's (2008) work on stock return predictability, we then demean the log D-O ratiov∼t=vt−v¯t$\widetilde{v}_t=v_t-\bar{v}_t$with a lower pre-2007 sample mean (v¯t,t<2007$\bar{v}_t,t<2007$) and a higher post-2007 sample mean (v¯t,t≥2007$\bar{v}_t,t\ge 2007$). Figure2plots the resulting demeaned seriesv∼t$\widetilde{v}_t$as the dashed line. This approach removes a low-frequency component from the D-O ratio, keeping the transitory variation. The resulting series is much less persistent than the original series (lowerϕT$\phi _T$).
  Figure8decomposes the variance of the transitory component of the D-O ratio. The transitory component of the D-O ratio explainsR2=32%$R^2= 32\%$of the variation in five-year cumulative surpluses. When the transitory component of the D-O ratio is high, surpluses tend to increase to push the D-O ratio back down. TableIVshows that future surpluses and returns combine to explain 26% and 52% of the variation in transitory component of D-O at the five-year and 10-year horizons, respectively, after bias correction. We can (narrowly) reject the null that fundamentals do not play a role at the 10-year horizon. This approach restores a role for fundamentals after accounting for the small-sample bias, but only in explaining the transitory variation in the D-O ratio.16
  This evidence shows that our main results, which find little evidence for surplus or return predictability, are to a substantial extent due to the low-frequency component in the D-O ratio.
  The variance decomposition of the previous section punts on the explanation for the large and persistent increase in the D-O ratio in the post-2007 sample. In the last part of the paper, we consider three candidate explanations for the structural break in the relationship between D-O and fundamentals. A first explanation for the structural break is biased beliefs about future surpluses, discussed in SectionII.B.1. SectionII.B.2explores the rise in foreign and central bank holdings of U.S. Treasurys as a second potential explanation. The bulk of the rise in these holdings comes after 2007, so the timing fits with the estimated time of the structural break. The third potential explanation is a change in fiscal and monetary policy regimes, discussed in SectionIII.B.3.
  We find a large Stambaugh bias in the fiscal predictability regressions. After we correct this bias, future government surpluses and discount rates do not explain variation in the level of the outstanding D-O ratio. One possible explanation for these results is that the U.S. government debt portfolio is mispriced because Treasury bond investors have become overly optimistic about the U.S. fiscal situation.
  AppendixEshows that this specification is consistent with a world in which the true primary surplus is i.i.d. but investors believe it contains a component that increases in lagged debt with slopebsubj>0$b^{subj}>0$. Assuming zero growth-adjusted returns, equation (13) then implies that investors believe that the log D-O ratio follows an AR(1) with persistence coefficient1−bsubj$1-b^{subj}$, whereas in truth the log D-O ratio follows a unit-root process.
  To assess this possibility empirically, we would ideally observe a time series of private-sector forecasts of future surpluses and future growth-adjusted returns over various horizons ranging from one to 10 years. Unfortunately, such data are unavailable. Private-sector survey evidence on the future surplus and GDP is available starting in 1994 from Consensus Economics, but only at the one-year-ahead horizon. However, the CBO predicts future surpluses 10 years out. Figure9shows that the one-year-ahead surplus forecast in both data sets is highly positively correlated (99.0%). If anything, the CBO's forecast is slightly more conservative.17This evidence motivates us to use the CBO forecast as a proxy for the private-sector forecast. Given the complex nature of surplus forecasts and the CBO's decades-long expertise, this strikes us as a reasonable assumption.
  The top panel of Figure10plots the CBO projectionsFt[vt+T]$\mathbb {F}_t [v_{t+T}]$against the realized time series for the D-O ratio from 1980 to 2022 (black line). The CBO reports five-year projections from 1980 to 1995 and 10-year projections starting in 1996. The projections at each projection date are indicated by a different color. The CBO has been consistently underpredicting the D-O ratio since the GFC. This was much less the case prior to the GFC. This bias arises mainly because the CBO has been systematically overpredicting future primary surpluses as a fraction of GDP since the GFC, as shown in the bottom panel. The GFC and the COVID-19 pandemic are large unanticipated shocks with a large fiscal impact. But the projections remain biased well after the GFC.18
  Figure11plots annualized forecast errors by comparing the CBO projected to the realized future surpluses. The forecast errors were close to zero from 1980 to 1997, −0.5% per year on average, but rose to 9.6% per annum from 1998 to 2022. The size of these forecast errors in the latter part of the sample is surprisingly large in light of the standard deviation of realized surplusesst$s_t$of 6.40% over the postwar period. Even well after the GFC, from 2010 to 2012, forecast errors are 5.4% per annum over the next 10 years.
  In sum, the CBO projections consistently impute too much mean reversion to the D-O ratio in the later part of the sample. Given the size and persistence of the CBO projection errors, and based on the evidence from one-year surplus forecast in Figure9, it is conceivable that private investors held similarly biased forecasts of future surpluses.
  To test this more formally, we reestimate the Campbell-Shiller decompositions of the D-O ratio under subjective beliefs. We construct a time series for the subjective cumulative surplus-to-GDP ratio, which is equal to the observed series from 1947 until 2006 but replaces the observed series with the CBO forecast after 2007. Given the average forecast error near zero in the first part of the sample and the large forecast error in the last part (recall Figure11), our procedure only adjusts the surplus when there is in fact a nonzero forecast error. Similarly, we construct a subjective expected future debt valueFtvt+T$\mathbb {F}_t v_{t+T}$from the observed data before 2007 and from the CBO projections after 2007. The subjective growth-adjusted returns are implied from the government budget constraint. We then reestimate the system of forecasting regressions in (4).
  Figure12plots the OLS regression results after bias correction. When using the subjective surpluses (red line), we find substantially more surplus predictability than we do using the actual surpluses throughout (blue line). At the 10-year horizon, 20.9% of the variance of the D-O ratio is attributed to variation in subjective future surpluses, compared to −5.8% for objective future surpluses. This difference is large given that only the last 16 observations of the independent variable differ between the red and blue lines. The bottom panel shows that at the 10-year horizon, future subjective D-O ratios explain 76.8% of the variation in the current D-O ratio, 24.6% points less than when using the objective future D-O ratio of our benchmark estimation. FigureD8shows that the results are similar when we use subjective surplus-to-output and D-O ratios starting from 1996 rather than from 2007.
  In sum, there is a stronger covariance between debt and future surpluses under subjective than under objective beliefs. This is due to forecast errors about the surplus that covary systematically and positively with the D-O ratio. There is a structural break in the wedge between objective and subjective beliefs in the last part of the sample, which imputes a persistent component in the D-O ratio. If investors' subjective expectations are aligned with CBO projections, an assumption supported in the data, they believe they live in a world with nontrivial mean reversion in the D-O ratio. But their systematic forecast errors, especially since the GFC, induce highly persistent shocks in the D-O ratio from the perspective of an econometrician.
  Foreign investors and the Federal Reserve Bank are considered to be relatively price-inelastic investors (Jiang, Krishnamurthy, and Lustig (2022)). Foreign holdings rise starting in the mid-1980s, while the holdings of the Federal Reserve Bank increase dramatically following the GFC and COVID-19 crises, when the Fed embarked on multiple rounds of quantitative easing. The increase in the size of central bank balance sheets since the GFC can be interpreted as a new wave of financial repression.21
  To examine the extent to which our results are driven by these holdings, we construct a measure of the D-O ratio that excludes the holdings of the Fed and the rest of the world. We denote the resulting ratio of the debt held by the domestic private sector and output byvtd$v^{d}_t$. Figure13shows that there has indeed been an increasing gap between domestic private-sector holdings of Treasury debt and the total amount of Treasury debt, especially since 2007. The resulting seriesvtd$v^{d}_t$bears similarity to the transitory component of the D-O ratiov∼t$\widetilde{v}_t$from the previous section in that it removes a persistent component of different magnitudes before and after 2007.
  We report the regression coefficients in FigureD9. The predictorvtd$v^{d}_{t}$explains around 40% of the variation in the future surpluses∑j=1Tρj−1st+j$\sum _{j=1}^T \rho ^{j-1} s_{t+j}$at the 10-year horizon after bias adjustment, similar to the evidence in Figure8from the structural break model. This result suggests that future surpluses may respond more to the debt that has to be absorbed by domestic investors, who have been found to be more price-elastic. However, after the bias correction, we still cannot reject the null that surpluses are not predictable.
  With surpluses not only not rising but actually falling in the wake of increases in debt, what enforces the government budget constraint? Corhay et al. (2023) suggest that the U.S. economy may have entered a “fiscal regime” where inflation, and not surpluses, adjust to restore the government budget constraint.24To test this possibility, we return to the analysis in TableIII. Specifically, we investigate how inflation responds to lagged debt before versus after 2007. The results in TableDIshow that inflation responds positively to lagged debt in the period after 2007, the opposite sign from the pre-2007 period. These findings are consistent with the fiscal regime of Corhay et al. (2023) prevailing in the post-2007 sample.
article.III. Conclusion:
The bond market's valuation of a claim to surpluses is surprisingly insensitive to news about future surpluses or returns from the perspective of an econometrician looking at the U.S. historical sample. This is a direct result of the D-O ratio's persistence. This persistence plagues small-sample predictability regressions that aim to uncover the extent to which high D-O episodes are followed by higher government surpluses, higher growth, higher inflation, or lower bond returns. After correcting the small-sample bias, there is no conclusive evidence in favor of such adjustments. These results, which show that there is no statistical evidence to conclude that the United States is on a fiscally sustainable path, stand in contrast with the existing literature.
  The evidence instead suggests that there is a large, highly persistent component in the D-O ratio. This component manifests as a structural break in the D-O ratio in 2007. We identify systematic forecast errors in primary surpluses as one potential source of high persistence in the D-O ratio. Evidence from fiscal projections suggests that bond investors may have been overpredicting future surpluses, especially since 2007, and hence may have anticipated mean reversion in the D-O ratio that failed to materialize. Even though the econometrician may not forecast larger surpluses when the D-O ratio rises, the bond market investor may. The portfolio of U.S. government debt may be persistently mispriced from the perspective of rational investors.
  A second potential source of such a structural break are Fed purchases of U.S Treasurys, which ramp up after 2007, a significant source of completely inelastic demand for Treasurys. Historically, governments have routinely resorted to central bank purchases when they increase spending in wartime, as just one example of financial repression. A third source we consider is a change in fiscal policy over time. We find evidence that the response coefficient of primary surpluses to lagged D-O becomes negative after 2007, indicating reduced fiscal conservatism.
  Finally, we cannot rule out the possibility of a peso event, a large fiscal correction not observed in our sample but anticipated and priced in by investors. Such fiscal adjustments might occur, for example, when the D-O ratio becomes very high (as assumed in Elenev et al. (2022)). However, there are a few reasons to be skeptical about this explanation. First, we have considered a long data sample spanning nearly two centuries and a diverse set of economic and political circumstances. Second, even the most sophisticated budget projections (which abstract from future fiscal corrections) have proven to be too optimistic hitherto. Third, fiscal adjustments when debt is high are politically difficult because they occur in high-marginal-utility states of the world.
  Editors: Antoinette Schoar, Urban Jermann, Leonid Kogan, Jonathan Lewellen, and Thomas Philippon
article.Appendix A: Linearizing the Budget Const: The rest of the derivation log-linearizes this expression, following the online appendix of Cochrane (2022a). Definevt=log(Vt/Yt)$v_t=\log (V_t/Y_t)$, whereYt$Y_t$is nominal gross domenstic product (GDP),rt=log(Rt)$r_t=\log (R_t)$is the log nominal return, andgt+πt=log(Yt/Yt−1)$g_t+\pi _t = \log (Y_t/Y_{t-1})$is nominal GDP growth, the sum of real GDP growth and inflation.
article.Appendix C: Unit-Root Tests:
ADF and PP tests have size distortions (Schwert (1989)). More problematically, they have low power against the null hypothesis ofI(0)$I(0)$alternatives that are close toI(1)$I(1)$. Simply put, unit-root tests cannot reliably distinguish highly persistent stationary processes from nonstationary processes. As a result, Kwiatkowski et al. (1992) propose that stationarity should be considered as the null hypothesis, and that the unit root should be the alternative. Rejection of the null of stationarity could then be viewed as convincing evidence in favor of a unit root. An alternative route that the literature has pursued is to improve the unit-root tests. Elliott, Rothenberg, and Stock (1996) and Ng and Perron (2001) develop “efficient unit root tests” to deal with near-unit-root alternatives.
  FigureC1shows the log D-O series in levels (top row) and in first differences (bottom row) alongside the sample autocorrelations for these series (right panels). In what follows, we set the deterministic time trend parameter toδ=0$\delta =0$based on a priori economic grounds.25The sample is annual data on the log D-O ratio from 1947 until 2022. The test results are summarized in TableCI.
  In general, the ADF and PP tests have low power against I(0) alternatives that are close to being I(1). Elliott, Rothenberg, and Stock (1996) (ERS) and Ng and Perron (2001) propose “efficient unit root” tests that have more power against persistent alternatives. ERS develop a “point optimal test” for the point alternativeϕ¯=1+c¯/T,c¯<0$\overline{\phi } = 1 + \overline{c}/T, \; \overline{c}<0$. ERS find that the power of the test is close to the power envelope (50% power) forc¯=−7$\overline{c}=-7$. We obtain an ERS point optimal test statistic of 15.81. The 5% (1%) critical value is 2.97 (1.87). This means we cannot reject the unit-root null. ERS also develop an efficient version of the ADFt$t$-test, called the DF-GLSt$t$-test. We obtain a DF-GLS test statistic of −1.19. The 5% (1%) critical value is −1.95 (−2.61). Again, this test does not reject the unit-root null. Finally, we implement the “modified efficient Phillips-Perron” test, due to Ng and Perron (2001). The test statistic is −1.08. Comparing to the critical values, we cannot reject the unit-root null.
  Panel B repeats the analysis on the prewar sample that starts in 1842 and goes until 1940. Chen et al. (2022) argue that the United States became the global safe-haven asset after WWII and entered in a new regime. Their analysis calls for treating the pre- and postwar samples separately. The statistical evidence for the prewar era is quite similar to that of the postwar era. We continue to fail to reject the null of a unit root. However, the null hypothesis of a stationary process for the log D-O ratio cannot be rejected at the 5% level either.
  Having considered the state-of-the-art univariate tests of the null of a unit root and the null of stationarity, we cannot reject either null hypothesis for the log D-O ratio. This conclusion is perhaps not surprising in light of the difficulties that univariate tests face in distinguishing a unit root from a near-unit-root process. The stock return predictability literature has uncovered similar issues when studying the persistence of the log dividend-price ratio on the aggregate stock market.
article.D.2. VAR Analysis:
We used a local projection method to evaluate the predictability of surplus and return in the main text. Alternatively, we can estimate a VAR system and roll it forward. As Cochrane (2011) emphasizes, long-run forecasts can be quite different from one-period forecasts, so imposing a VAR structure is restrictive. That said, and as a robustness check, we study the implications of a VAR model in which we construct long-run regression coefficients from their implied one-period versions.
  We estimate (D1) to (D3) and then construct the implied long-horizon predictability coefficients. We use our bootstrap procedure to compute standard errors on the short- and long-horizon regression coefficients and to compute small-sample biases.
  FigureD10plots the bias-adjusted long-horizon (i) direct regression coefficients from local projections and (ii) VAR-implied coefficients for horizons from 1 to 10 years. The two sets of estimates are similar, though the VAR-implied coefficients have larger standard errors.
article.Appendix E: Biased Beliefs: Equations (13) to (15) relate the debt dynamics under the econometrician's measure to that under investors' measures. More specifically, we can specify the stochastic process for the primary surplus under both measures, and derive the corresponding debt dynamics. For simplicity, we abstract from growth-adjusted returns by settingr∼t+j=0$\widetilde{r}_{t+j} = 0$under both objective and subjective measures.
